titleStmt.title: Memorandum of Conversation, by the Deputy Assistant Secretary for Far Eastern Affairs (Johnson)

head.hi: Memorandum of Conversation, by the Deputy Assistant Secretary for Far Eastern Affairs (Johnson)

list.head: Subject:
list.item: Ambassador Panikkar’s Action in Peiping on Prisoner of War Issue

list.head: Participants:
list.item: Mr. F. S. Tomlinson, Counselor, British Embassy
Mr. D. A. Greenhill, First Secretary, British Embassy
Mr. U. Alexis Johnson, Deputy Assistant Secretary
Mr. Kenneth T. Young, Jr., Director, Office of Northeast Asian Affairs

div.p:
Mr. Tomlinson today informed me of a message from the UK High Commissioner in New Delhi which confirmed that Panikkar had transmitted the first Eden rough draft formula on prisoners of war to the Vice Minister of Foreign Affairs at Peking,In a memorandum of conversation, by the Counselor of the Embassy in New Delhi, transmitted to the Department of State as despatch 2968 from New Delhi, June 5, 1952, Counselor Drumright passed on the following information which he received from the acting British High Commissioner: “The Chinese Vice Minister of Foreign Affairs had indicated that he personally felt that the five-point approach might have some chance of acceptance, but it would have to be considered by Chou En-lai.” (795.00/6–552) with a prefatory statement to the effect that the GOI was maintaining close contact with other governments to evolve a formula on the prisoner-of-war issue which might meet the point of view of the Chinese Communist Government. (According to the telegram it appeared that Panikkar’s prefatory statement could well be interpreted by the Chinese as indicating that the formula had the approval of the UK and possibly even the US Governments.) In addition, Panikkar had entirely on his own initiative put forward the suggestion that the rescreening of prisoners of war take place in some “neutral territory” and in reply to the Vice Minister’s question had suggested Kaesong. Panikkar pointed out that this part of his proposal
did not commit the Government of India and that it had not been seen by any other government.
Mr. Tomlinson stated that comments of the Foreign Office had not yet been received and that he would get in touch with me again as soon as they came in.

Mr. Tomlinson and I agreed that this action by Panikkar appeared to have greatly diminished prospects for any constructive use of the Government of India channel for arriving at any resolution of the POW question.
